label: MT-Bench
definition: A benchmark of challenging multi-turn questions that grades chat models on open-ended quality, with a strong model acting as the judge; it measures conversational helpfulness and instruction following rather than the multiple-choice knowledge that benchmarks like MMLU test.

body.text: MT-Bench is a benchmark built to measure how good a model is as a conversational assistant, a quality that multiple-choice knowledge tests do not capture. It consists of a curated set of demanding prompts spread across categories such as writing, roleplay, reasoning, math, coding, and information extraction, and every prompt has two turns: an initial request followed by a related follow-up. The two-turn structure is deliberate, because a model that answers a single question well may still lose the thread when the conversation continues, and following instructions across turns is central to being a useful assistant.

The benchmark exists because the qualities that make a chat model good, helpfulness, coherence, depth, tone, and faithful instruction following, have no answer key. A factual benchmark like mmlu can be graded by string-matching a letter choice, but there is no fixed correct response to "write a persuasive email" or "help me debug this and then refactor it." MT-Bench addresses this by scoring with an llm-as-judge: a strong model reads each response and rates it, typically on a scale of one to ten, against the prompt and the conversation so far. This made open-ended evaluation cheap and reproducible enough to run on every model iteration, where relying on human raters would have been far slower and costlier.

MT-Bench was introduced together with the research that established judge-model evaluation as credible, and it serves as the single-model, scored complement to a head-to-head arena. Where chatbot-arena collects human pairwise votes and aggregates them into an elo-rating, MT-Bench hands a fixed battery of hard prompts to one model at a time and produces an absolute average score from the judge, so a model can be assessed without waiting for a crowd of human comparisons to accumulate. The two are often reported side by side, the arena capturing real human preference and MT-Bench giving a fast, repeatable proxy.

Its limitations are those of its judge and its fixed prompt set. Because grading is done by a model, MT-Bench inherits the biases of llm-as-judge, including a tilt toward longer or more confident answers and possible self-preference when the judge and the contestant share a lineage, and the absolute scores are only as well-calibrated as the judge itself, a concern that connects to calibration. The prompt set is small and public, so it can saturate as models improve and is exposed to benchmark-contamination if its questions leak into training data. It remains a widely used quick gauge of multi-turn conversational quality, best read alongside human-grounded measures rather than on its own.
